title: Bread and Butter Pudding

overview.description: A classic British pudding made from buttered bread, dried fruit and a rich vanilla custard, baked until golden and softly set.

method: Grease a large ovenproof dish with a little butter.
Butter the slices of bread generously on one side.
Cut the bread into triangles or quarters.
Arrange half of the bread in the dish, buttered side up.
Scatter over half of the raisins and chopped apricots.
Add the remaining bread and scatter over the remaining dried fruit.
Warm the milk and cream gently in a saucepan until steaming but not boiling.
In a bowl, whisk together the eggs, caster sugar, vanilla, cinnamon, lemon zest and salt.
Gradually whisk the warm milk mixture into the eggs.
Pour the custard slowly over the bread.
Press the bread down gently so it absorbs some of the custard.
Sprinkle the demerara sugar over the top.
Bake for 40–50 minutes until golden brown and just set in the centre.
Allow to rest for 10 minutes before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Bread and butter pudding evolved from older British ways of using stale bread and is closely related to earlier baked bread puddings.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How stale bread absorbs a custard mixture and how gentle baking transforms it into a soft, rich pudding with a golden top.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A low-cost, practical pudding that turns leftover bread into something special and uses ingredients already commonly kept in the pantry.
chapman-tips: Slightly stale bread absorbs the custard better than very fresh bread.
Don't boil the milk and cream mixture.
Pour the custard over the bread slowly so it soaks evenly.
Allowing the pudding to stand before baking improves the texture.
The centre should be just set rather than completely firm.
Brioche can be used for a richer version, although ordinary white bread works extremely well.
The apricot can be omitted or replaced with extra raisins.
A little grated nutmeg is also excellent in the custard.
The demerara sugar gives the top a lovely caramelised crunch.
notes.batch-cooking: Better fresh. It can be reheated, although the texture is best on the day it is baked.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently and serve with custard.
